title: Basal Accretion Along the South Central Chilean Margin and Its Relationship to Great Earthquakes
journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
abstract: In 2017 seismic reflection data were acquired along approximately 1,000 km of the south-central Chile margin using the R/V Langseth's 15 km long receiver array and 108.2 liter seismic source. A thick underthrust sediment layer separated from downgoing oceanic crust is proposed to be the source of strong coupling producing the world's largest earthquakes. Basal accretion of crustal slices from the subducting Nazca plate forms anomalous high-velocity wedge bodies at depth.

location.description: South-central Chile margin from ~30°S to 44°S (CEVICHE survey area) encompassing both 1960 and 2010 earthquake rupture zones

data.notes: 15.15-km long-offset MCS reflection data from 2017 CEVICHE cruise along ~1000 km of south-central Chile; images underthrust sediment layer, basal accretion of crustal slices, and plate boundary reflectivity
Raw multibeam swath bathymetry data (1,753 files, ~201 GB, Kongsberg EM122) from the 2017 CEVICHE (MGL1701) cruise, offshore south-central Chile 30°–44°S.
Raw underway gravimeter data from the 2017 CEVICHE (MGL1701) cruise, offshore south-central Chile.
Raw magnetometer data from the 2017 CEVICHE (MGL1701) cruise, offshore south-central Chile.
Primary navigation (GPS positions, 253 MB) from the 2017 CEVICHE (MGL1701) cruise, archived via R2R.

_data_note: MGL1701 CEVICHE cruise data accessible via MGDS and RVData. Long-offset 15.15-km streamer makes this a unique dataset for imaging deep structure.

processing.summary: Long-offset MCS reflection data (15.15-km streamer) from the 2017 R/V Langseth MGL1701 CEVICHE cruise were processed using full waveform inversion and prestack depth migration. The processing reveals a thick underthrust sediment layer at the plate boundary and anomalous high-velocity wedge bodies interpreted as basally accreted crustal slices from the subducting Nazca plate. The data cover ~1,000 km of the south-central Chile margin (30°–44°S).
workflow: 4. Velocity analysis and tomographic model building
6. Full waveform inversion for velocity model refinement
processing.notes: 15.15-km streamer. Processing used Paradigm Echos and Geodepth (confirmed from Acknowledgments). LIFT multiple suppression (Choo et al. 2004) used as second step after SRME.

analysis_notes: Validated against PDF. Software corrected: ProMAX/FWI → Paradigm Echos + Paradigm Geodepth (confirmed from Acknowledgments). Source volume 108.2 L = 6,600 in³ confirmed. Data available from MGDS (marine-geo.org). Not open access (Cochrane Chile institutional download). Morgan's first name 'Joanna' could not be confirmed from PDF (shows J. K. only).
